critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design. Let me work through the potential issues systematically, organized by root cause.

## Root Cause 1: The Baseline Is Not Truly Comparable — Feature Access Asymmetry Remains

**Claim:** Both models receive the same structured auxiliary features, so any transformer gain is attributable to text understanding.

**Problem:** The transformer receives full email body text (up to 2048 tokens) while the baseline receives only TF-IDF on sender domain and subject line (max 500 features). This is explicitly stated and framed as intentional ('true baseline representing status quo'), but then the confound-control section claims 'both transformer and baseline receive the same structured features' — these two statements are in tension. The baseline does NOT receive equivalent text access. The design is testing 'transformer with full text + structured features' vs. 'structured features + subject/domain TF-IDF.' Any observed gain is confounded by text access difference, not just modeling capacity.

The proposed ablation (removing body text from transformer) would partially address this, but it's described as optional ('would additionally...'), not required. Without this ablation being mandatory and reported, the primary comparison cannot attribute gains to 'text understanding' vs. 'access to full body text as a feature.'

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Run a third condition: bag-of-words or TF-IDF on full body text + same structured features as baseline, no transformer. If this third condition closes most of the gap, text access — not transformer architecture — is the explanatory variable.

## Root Cause 2: Campaign Clustering May Be Incomplete, Creating Residual Leakage

**Claim:** Campaigns are identified by sender domain + similar subject lines, and <0.1% overlap validates isolation.

**Problem:** Phishing campaigns frequently vary sender domain deliberately (compromised accounts, domain spoofing, lookalike domains) while keeping body text, URLs, or template structure nearly identical. Two emails from a single campaign might have completely different sender domains and only loosely similar subjects but share a payload URL, HTML template fingerprint, or credential harvesting landing page. The clustering key (sender domain + subject hash) would correctly treat these as separate campaigns and allow them to span train and test. This means the campaign isolation guarantee is weaker than stated — it catches only campaigns with consistent sender identity, not campaigns using distributed sending infrastructure.

The 0.1% overlap validation checks only the clustering key used to define campaigns, so it can confirm that the grouping rule was applied correctly but cannot detect campaign variants that happen to use different sender domains.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Compute URL domain overlap between train and test sets (what fraction of test malicious emails contain a URL whose domain appeared in a train malicious email?). If this is substantially higher than the sender domain + subject overlap rate, the campaign leakage concern is real. Similarly, check body text n-gram similarity between train and test malicious emails after campaign grouping.

## Root Cause 3: Macro-Averaging Over Campaigns Introduces Metric Instability for Small Campaigns

**Claim:** Campaign-level macro-averaged AUROC prevents high-volume campaigns from dominating the score.

**Problem:** AUROC within a campaign cluster is undefined or degenerate when a campaign has only one class represented (e.g., a small benign sender group with zero malicious emails, or a single-email phishing campaign). For campaigns with very few emails (say 5–20), within-campaign AUROC has enormous variance — it's essentially a ranking over a tiny set. Macro-averaging over hundreds of such campaigns means the primary metric is dominated by noise from small campaigns. This could make model selection unstable: the 'better' model on macro-AUROC might not be better on the metrics that actually matter operationally.

This is a real problem because phishing campaigns can be very small (targeted BEC is sometimes a single email) and benign sender groups can be large or small. The distribution of campaign sizes is almost certainly highly skewed.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Report the distribution of campaign sizes in the test set. Compute the primary metric both as macro-averaged campaign AUROC and as standard AUROC on all test emails. If these two numbers diverge substantially (e.g., >3 AUROC points), the metric design is materially affecting the apparent comparison between models.

## Root Cause 4: Auxiliary Feature Overlap With Transformer Input Creates Implicit Redundancy That Could Mask What the Transformer Actually Learns

**Claim:** Fusing auxiliary features with [CLS] embedding via concatenation + dense projection tests whether text understanding adds value beyond structured metadata.

**Problem:** Several auxiliary features are derivable from content that also appears in the transformer's input sequence. SPF/DKIM results, Received hop count, and header structure are in the SMTP headers fed to the transformer. URL counts and suspicious TLD presence may be inferable from URL text in the body. If the transformer learns to encode these signals from text (which a fine-tuned BERT-class model likely would, given sufficient training data), the auxiliary feature vector is partially redundant with the [CLS] embedding. This isn't a leakage problem — it won't inflate performance — but it means the ablation comparing 'transformer + aux features' vs. 'transformer - body text' won't cleanly isolate what the transformer contributes, because the dense projection layer may learn to up-weight aux features and down-weight [CLS] when body text is removed, making the ablation results hard to interpret.

This is more of a interpretability concern than a validity concern for the primary comparison, so it's lower severity.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Inspect learned weights or attention patterns after training. If [CLS] embedding contribution to the final classification decision is small relative to auxiliary features, the transformer is functioning primarily as a regularized metadata model, not a text model.

## Root Cause 5: Labeling Lag Interacts With Campaign Grouping in a Potentially Biased Way

**Claim:** The ≥7-day labeling delay prevents future information from influencing training labels.

**Problem:** The 7-day lag is designed to prevent post-send events (sandbox detonation, user click confirmation) from leaking into features. But it creates a selection bias: emails that are quickly and unambiguously malicious (e.g., matching a known malware hash at receipt time) will have labels available at 7 days, while ambiguous BEC or credential-harvesting emails that require analyst investigation might take weeks or months to confirm. The included set is therefore enriched for 'easier' or 'already-known' malicious emails. Novel, sophisticated attacks that require extended investigation — exactly the cases where a transformer might provide the most value — are systematically excluded. This means the experiment may understate the difficulty of the actual production problem and overstate performance on novel threats.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Report the distribution of label confirmation times (time from receipt to confirmed label). If the median confirmation time for malicious emails is close to 7 days, most labels are included and bias is minimal. If a large fraction of malicious emails take >30 days to confirm, the excluded tail may contain the hardest and most operationally important cases.

## Summary Assessment

Issues 1 and 2 are the most methodologically significant. Issue 1 (feature access asymmetry) means the primary comparison cannot distinguish 'transformer architecture' from 'access to full body text' without the body-text ablation being run and reported as a required result. Issue 2 (incomplete campaign clustering) means the campaign isolation guarantee may not hold for campaigns using distributed sending infrastructure, which is common for sophisticated phishing. Issue 3 (metric instability from small campaigns) could affect model selection validity. Issues 4 and 5 are real but less likely to change the experimental conclusions.

The design is careful and better than most in this domain. Issue 1 is fixable by promoting the ablation to required; Issue 2 is fixable by adding URL-domain overlap as a secondary leakage check. If those two are addressed, the methodology is substantially sound.
all_issues_raised: The baseline lacks access to full email body text while the transformer has it, so observed performance gaps cannot be attributed to 'text understanding' vs. 'text access' without a mandatory full-body TF-IDF ablation condition.

Campaign clustering on sender domain + subject hash misses campaigns that vary sender identity while sharing payload URLs or HTML templates, leaving residual cross-partition leakage detectable only via URL domain overlap analysis.

Macro-averaging AUROC over campaign clusters of highly variable size creates a primary metric dominated by variance from tiny campaigns, which could make model selection unstable and diverge from operationally meaningful performance.

Auxiliary features partially overlap with content visible in the transformer's input sequence, making the planned body-text ablation ambiguous to interpret because the dense projection layer can compensate by reweighting features.

The ≥7-day labeling lag systematically excludes long-investigation malicious emails (likely novel BEC and sophisticated phishing), enriching the dataset toward easier, already-known attacks and potentially overstating performance on novel threats.

Campaign clustering uses approximate subject matching for grouping but exact subject hash for leakage verification, creating a gap where near-duplicate campaign variants can cross train–test boundaries without being caught.
The baseline lacks full body text, making it impossible to attribute transformer gains to architecture vs. raw information advantage without the (currently optional) full-text ablation.

Macro-averaging AUROC across campaigns with highly variable sizes (including single-email BEC campaigns) produces a high-variance, hard-to-interpret metric that is most degenerate exactly for targeted threats where text understanding is hypothesized to matter most.

Training an 'email-specific vocabulary' from scratch while using pretrained weights creates a silent misconfiguration risk where the embedding layer is decoupled from pretrained representations, causing underperformance that may not be apparent on easy bulk-spam cases.

Reputation-based features (domain age, RBL status) measured at dataset construction time rather than email receipt time introduce temporal leakage that inflates baseline performance on historical emails.

The baseline lacks access to full email body text while the transformer receives it, making it impossible to distinguish gains from representation learning vs. gains from simply having access to more text — a full-text TF-IDF baseline is required to decompose this.

Campaign clustering based on sender domain + subject hash systematically fails to group singleton and spear-phishing/BEC emails, creating potential soft leakage among the highest-value threat class that the 0.1% overlap threshold cannot detect.

Blocklist membership and malware hash features may be extracted from current-state feeds rather than point-in-time receipt logs, causing temporal contamination of auxiliary features even though the label delay is correctly enforced.

Macro-averaging AUROC across campaign clusters gives high weight to small or singleton clusters with very few positives, producing a primary metric that may reflect sampling variance rather than model quality differences.

## Root Cause 1: The Baseline Is Not Truly Comparable — Feature Access Asymmetry Remains

If this is substantially higher than the sender domain + subject overlap rate, the campaign leakage concern is real. Similarly, check body text n-gram similarity between train and test malicious emails after campaign grouping.

## Root Cause 3: Macro-Averaging Over Campaigns Introduces Metric Instability for Small Campaigns

**Claim:** Campaign-level macro-averaged AUROC prevents high-volume campaigns from dominating the score.

**Problem:** AUROC within a campaign cluster is undefined or degenerate when a campaign has only one class represented (e.g., a small benign sender group with zero malicious emails, or a single-email phishing campaign). For campaigns with very few emails (say 5–20), within-campaign AUROC has enormous variance — it's essentially a ranking over a tiny set. Macro-averaging over hundreds of such campaigns means the primary metric is dominated by noise from small campaigns. This could make model selection unstable: the 'better' model on macro-AUROC might not be better on the metrics that actually matter operationally.

This is a real problem because phishing campaigns can be very small (targeted BEC is sometimes a single email) and benign sender groups can be large or small. The distribution of campaign sizes is almost certainly highly skewed.

Compute the primary metric both as macro-averaged campaign AUROC and as standard AUROC on all test emails. If these two numbers diverge substantially (e.g., >3 AUROC points), the metric design is materially affecting the apparent comparison between models.

## Root Cause 4: Auxiliary Feature Overlap With Transformer Input Creates Implicit Redundancy That Could Mask What the Transformer Actually Learns

**Claim:** Fusing auxiliary features with [CLS] embedding via concatenation + dense projection tests whether text understanding adds value beyond structured metadata.

**Problem:** Several auxiliary features are derivable from content that also appears in the transformer's input sequence. If the transformer learns to encode these signals from text (which a fine-tuned BERT-class model likely would, given sufficient training data), the auxiliary feature vector is partially redundant with the [CLS] embedding. If [CLS] embedding contribution to the final classification decision is small relative to auxiliary features, the transformer is functioning primarily as a regularized metadata model, not a text model.

**Claim:** The ≥7-day labeling delay prevents future information from influencing training labels.

**Problem:** The 7-day lag is designed to prevent post-send events (sandbox detonation, user click confirmation) from leaking into features. But it creates a selection bias: emails that are quickly and unambiguously malicious (e.g., matching a known malware hash at receipt time) will have labels available at 7 days, while ambiguous BEC or credential-harvesting emails that require analyst investigation might take weeks or months to confirm. The included set is therefore enriched for 'easier' or 'already-known' malicious emails. **Evidence that would resolve this:** Report the distribution of label confirmation times (time from receipt to confirmed label). If the median confirmation time for malicious emails is close to 7 days, most labels are included and bias is minimal. If a large fraction of malicious emails take >30 days to confirm, the excluded tail may contain the hardest and most operationally important cases.

## Summary Assessment

Issues 1 and 2 are the most methodologically significant. Issue 1 (feature access asymmetry) means the primary comparison cannot distinguish 'transformer architecture' from 'access to full body text' without the body-text ablation being run and reported as a required result. Issue 2 (incomplete campaign clustering) means the campaign isolation guarantee may not hold for campaigns using distributed sending infrastructure, which is common for sophisticated phishing. Issue 3 (metric instability from small campaigns) could affect model selection validity. Issues 4 and 5 are real but less likely to change the experimental conclusions.

The design is careful and better than most in this domain. Issue 1 is fixable by promoting the ablation to required; Issue 2 is fixable by adding URL-domain overlap as a secondary leakage check. If those two are addressed, the methodology is substantially sound.

issues_raised: Campaign clustering uses approximate subject matching for grouping but exact subject hash for leakage verification, creating a gap where near-duplicate campaign variants can cross train–test boundaries without being caught.
The baseline lacks full body text, making it impossible to attribute transformer gains to architecture vs. raw information advantage without the (currently optional) full-text ablation.

Macro-averaging AUROC across campaigns with highly variable sizes (including single-email BEC campaigns) produces a high-variance, hard-to-interpret metric that is most degenerate exactly for targeted threats where text understanding is hypothesized to matter most.

Training an 'email-specific vocabulary' from scratch while using pretrained weights creates a silent misconfiguration risk where the embedding layer is decoupled from pretrained representations, causing underperformance that may not be apparent on easy bulk-spam cases.

Reputation-based features (domain age, RBL status) measured at dataset construction time rather than email receipt time introduce temporal leakage that inflates baseline performance on historical emails.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
Overall this is a carefully designed experiment with sensible controls. I'll work through the genuine methodological concerns I can identify, organized by root cause.

---

**Root Cause 1: Campaign clustering is under-defined and potentially leaky**

The methodology defines campaign clusters by 'sender domain + similar subject lines (hash-based binning or SOC-assigned campaign ID).' The leakage check is also defined as <0.1% of test emails sharing 'sender domain + subject hash' with train. These two definitions are almost certainly not the same thing: 'similar subject lines' (approximate matching) is used for grouping, but exact 'subject hash' is used for the leakage check. A sophisticated phishing campaign that rotates subject lines slightly but comes from the same infrastructure will be grouped correctly if SOC assigns a campaign ID, but if that ID is absent and hash-based binning is used, near-duplicate variants may be split across train and test. The leakage check would then pass (no exact subject hash match) while genuine signal leakage exists (near-duplicate body text, shared URLs, identical sender domain). The mechanism of failure: the transformer, fine-tuned on exact text, can memorize campaign-specific phrasing without that being caught by the hash-based leakage check. Evidence: compute body-text similarity (e.g., MinHash or character 4-gram Jaccard) between train and test malicious emails; if >5% of test malicious emails have >0.8 Jaccard similarity to a training email, leakage is present.

**Root Cause 2: Asymmetric feature access between transformer and baseline undermines the stated comparison**

The design claims 'both transformer and baseline receive the same structured features.' But the transformer's input includes full email body text while the baseline does not — this is explicitly acknowledged and justified as 'represents status quo.' The problem is that the stated research question ('whether full text + fine-tuning genuinely outperforms hand-crafted features') conflates two confounders: (a) the transformer architecture's representational capacity and (b) the transformer's access to a strictly richer input signal (full body text vs. TF-IDF on subject+domain, 500 features). Any observed gain could come from (b) alone — a bag-of-words logistic regression on full body text would have the same informational advantage. Without a logistic regression + full body text TF-IDF baseline, the experiment cannot attribute gains to the transformer's architecture vs. to the additional raw signal. The design mentions an ablation (removing body text from transformer), which would help bound the text contribution, but this ablation is described as optional ('would additionally'), not as a required condition of the comparison. Evidence: add a logistic regression trained on full body text TF-IDF (e.g., 50k features) + same auxiliary features; if it matches or exceeds the transformer's AUROC, the gain is not architectural.

**Root Cause 3: Campaign-macro-averaged AUROC conceals the operationally relevant failure mode**

Macro-averaging AUROC across campaigns treats a single-email campaign (e.g., a highly targeted BEC with one instance) identically to a 10,000-email campaign. For a single-email campaign, AUROC is either 0 or 1 depending on the rank of that one email — it becomes a binary variable. If test contains many small campaigns (which is likely in BEC/targeted scenarios), the macro-averaged AUROC is dominated by these binary outcomes and becomes high-variance and hard to interpret. More importantly, BEC and targeted spear-phishing are exactly the threats where text understanding is hypothesized to matter most, yet these are precisely the campaigns where AUROC per campaign is most degenerate. The metric design thus has the most noise in the most theoretically interesting subpopulation. Evidence: report the distribution of campaign sizes in the test set; if >30% of campaigns have <5 emails, macro-averaged AUROC is not a reliable summary statistic for this comparison.

**Root Cause 4: Tokenizer trained on email data may transfer poorly from general pretrained vocabulary**

The design states 'email-specific vocabulary' is learned from train text only and the pretrained model (DistilBERT/RoBERTa) is unfrozen for fine-tuning. If the email-specific vocabulary differs substantially from the pretrained model's vocabulary (i.e., a new tokenizer is trained rather than using the pretrained tokenizer), the pretrained weights are no longer aligned with the new token embeddings — effectively the model is trained from scratch on 50M emails, which is almost certainly insufficient to learn good representations compared to the 160GB of text used to pretrain RoBERTa. If the pretrained tokenizer is reused (the more sensible choice), the phrase 'email-specific vocabulary' is misleading. This is a silent misconfiguration risk: if someone trains a new tokenizer on email data and initializes the embedding layer randomly, the transformer will underperform relative to a properly initialized model, but it will still produce plausible-looking AUROC values on easy cases (bulk spam where URL features already carry the signal), making the misconfiguration hard to detect without ablation. Evidence: confirm that the tokenizer vocabulary and embedding weights are directly inherited from the pretrained checkpoint, with at most a vocabulary extension for domain-specific tokens (with randomly initialized new embeddings, warm-started); report vocabulary overlap between pretrained and email-corpus tokenizers.

**Root Cause 5: 7-day labeling delay does not fully eliminate post-send feature leakage**

The design uses a ≥7-day labeling delay to prevent labels from being influenced by post-send events. However, some auxiliary features (malware hash database matches, RBL blocklist status, domain age at time of *labeling* vs. time of *receipt*) may themselves be measured at pipeline construction time rather than at email receipt time. Specifically: if domain age is computed when the dataset is built (18–24 months after receipt for older emails), it reflects the domain's current age, not its age at delivery. A phishing domain registered 2 days before sending will look 2 years old when measured at dataset construction. Similarly, RBL presence may reflect current blocklist status rather than status at delivery. For the baseline, which heavily weights sender reputation features, this could inflate performance on historical malicious emails (the domain is now definitively blocked), producing an overestimate of what the baseline could have achieved in real-time deployment. Evidence: compare model performance on emails received in the most recent 3-month window (where measurement-time ≈ receipt-time) vs. older emails; if baseline AUROC is substantially higher on older emails, temporal feature leakage is present.

---

**Summary of genuine issues:**

Issues 2 (asymmetric feature access making the architectural contribution unidentifiable without the optional ablation), 3 (degenerate per-campaign AUROC for small campaigns dominating the macro-average in the most interesting subpopulation), and 5 (temporal feature leakage in reputation-heavy baseline features) are the most fundamental. Issue 1 (approximate campaign leakage check) is real but probably modest in effect. Issue 4 is a silent misconfiguration risk worth flagging.

None of these individually invalidate the experiment, but issues 2 and 3 together mean the primary comparison cannot cleanly answer the stated research question without the ablation being promoted from optional to required, and the metric being supplemented with volume-weighted AUROC or per-campaign size stratification.

issues_raised: The baseline lacks access to full email body text while the transformer receives it, making it impossible to distinguish gains from representation learning vs. gains from simply having access to more text — a full-text TF-IDF baseline is required to decompose this.

Campaign clustering based on sender domain + subject hash systematically fails to group singleton and spear-phishing/BEC emails, creating potential soft leakage among the highest-value threat class that the 0.1% overlap threshold cannot detect.

Blocklist membership and malware hash features may be extracted from current-state feeds rather than point-in-time receipt logs, causing temporal contamination of auxiliary features even though the label delay is correctly enforced.

Macro-averaging AUROC across campaign clusters gives high weight to small or singleton clusters with very few positives, producing a primary metric that may reflect sampling variance rather than model quality differences.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
## Overall Assessment

This is a carefully designed experiment. The authors have anticipated most of the standard pitfalls in email security ML: campaign leakage, label contamination, future information, class imbalance, and evaluation inflation from volume-dominated metrics. The scope statement at the end is appropriately humble. I'll focus on the residual genuine issues.

---

## Issue 1: The Baseline Is Systematically Disadvantaged by Text Access, Not Just Feature Engineering Philosophy

**Claim:** The baseline 'represents the status quo in deployed email security systems' and the comparison tests whether full text plus fine-tuning outperforms hand-crafted features.

**Why this is problematic:** The baseline receives TF-IDF on subject line and sender domain only (500 features total across both), while the transformer sees the full email body up to 2048 tokens. The methodology frames this as 'the baseline doesn't get full text' as if that's a design choice reflecting real-world constraints, not an experimental asymmetry. But the actual question being tested — 'does text understanding help?' — requires either (a) a baseline that also gets full-text TF-IDF/n-gram features so you isolate the representation learning contribution, or (b) an explicit acknowledgment that 'access to full body text' and 'deep representation' are being tested simultaneously and cannot be decomposed. The ablation mentioned in Section 8 (removing body text from the transformer) is exactly what's needed, but it's described as optional rather than required for the central claim. If the transformer gains 5 AUROC points but most of that gain comes from seeing the full body as any bag-of-words would, the conclusion 'fine-tuned transformers outperform engineered features' is true but misleading — the mechanism is text access, not representation quality.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Add a third model: logistic regression or gradient boosted trees on full-body TF-IDF (unigrams + bigrams, 10k–50k features) plus the same auxiliary features. If this full-text baseline matches or beats the transformer, the gain is from text access. If the transformer still wins by a meaningful margin, the gain is from representation learning. The Section 8 ablation is necessary but not sufficient — you need an external baseline with full text, not just a crippled transformer.

---

## Issue 2: Campaign Clustering Is Defined Circularly and May Fail on Novel Attack Patterns

**Claim:** Campaign isolation is achieved by grouping emails with matching sender domain and similar subject lines, ensuring phishing variants don't cross train–test boundaries.

**Why this is problematic:** The very emails that matter most for evaluating transformer text understanding — novel, low-volume, well-crafted spear-phishing or BEC attempts — are precisely the ones that do *not* cluster by sender domain + subject hash. A BEC email impersonating the CFO arrives from a lookalike domain with a unique subject; it won't form a campaign cluster under this definition and will be treated as a singleton. These singletons get assigned to train/val/test independently of each other. If two very similar BEC attempts happen to land in both train and test (same lookalike domain pattern, similar subject phrasing, but not identical enough to hash-bin together), you have soft leakage on the highest-value threat class. Separately, the 0.1% overlap threshold on sender domain + subject hash is the *verification* metric for the clustering, not a guarantee — it only confirms the clustering rule worked for cases it was designed to catch, not for structural variants it misses by design.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Report campaign isolation metrics separately for high-volume clustered campaigns vs. singletons (emails with no cluster mates). Check whether the singleton malicious emails in the test set share any n-gram fingerprints, URL domains, or IP-derived features with singleton malicious emails in training. If singleton test performance is meaningfully higher than campaign-level test performance, leakage among unclustered emails is a plausible explanation.

---

## Issue 3: The 7-Day Labeling Lag Controls Future Leakage into Labels but Not into Features

**Claim:** The ≥7-day labeling delay ensures the model never trains on labels influenced by post-send events not available at inference time.

**Why this is problematic:** The label delay prevents future events from contaminating the *target*, but several auxiliary features listed are implicitly dynamic and may reflect post-receipt intelligence at the time of feature extraction. Specifically: (1) Known malware hash database matches — if the feature extraction is run once at experiment time (not at email receipt time), hashes confirmed malicious after a delay will yield match=1 even though at receipt time the hash was unknown. (2) RBL/blocklist presence for sender reputation — blocklists are updated continuously; a domain blocked on day 8 would show as blocked when features are extracted on day 30, but was clean at receipt. (3) Domain age: stable. (4) SPF/DKIM: stable. The severity depends entirely on *when* feature extraction is performed. If it's done at experiment build time from current state rather than from logged-at-receipt state, the malware hash and blocklist features are temporally contaminated — the transformer's auxiliary features carry post-receipt signal even if the label was delayed.

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Confirm that blocklist membership and malware hash matches are pulled from point-in-time snapshots logged at email receipt, not from the current state of those feeds at dataset construction time. If point-in-time logs are unavailable, these features should be excluded or treated as suspect. An indirect test: compare the malware hash match rate between the earliest 20% and latest 20% of emails in the training set — systematic differences in match rates for historically old emails would signal retrospective contamination.

---

## Issue 4: Macro-Averaging AUROC Across Campaign Clusters Has a Subtle Small-Sample Instability Problem

**Claim:** Campaign-level macro-averaging prevents high-volume campaigns from dominating the score and makes the metric reflect broad threat coverage.

**Why this is problematic:** Many campaign clusters — especially singletons or small BEC threads — will appear in the test set with very few positive examples (possibly 1–3 malicious emails). AUROC is undefined for clusters with only one class present, and highly unstable for clusters with 1–3 positives. Macro-averaging over these clusters will give enormous weight to noisy, near-undefined AUROCs from small clusters, potentially making the aggregate metric more reflective of sampling variance than of model quality. The methodology does not describe how single-class or small-cluster AUROCs are handled (excluded? imputed? included at face value?).

**Evidence that would resolve this:** Report the distribution of per-cluster AUROC values (median, IQR, and the fraction of clusters excluded for having only one class). Also report a micro-AUROC (standard) alongside the macro-AUROC and check whether model rankings are stable between the two. If rankings flip between micro and macro, the macro metric is dominated by small-cluster instability rather than genuine coverage differences.

---

## Summary

Issues 1 and 3 share a root cause: the experiment cannot currently decompose *what* the transformer gains over the baseline (text access vs. representation vs. temporally contaminated features). Issues 2 and 4 share a root cause: the campaign-centric methodology was designed for high-volume clustered attacks and has unexamined failure modes for the low-volume, high-value threat class (singletons) that arguably matters most.

Issue 1 is the most significant: without a full-text shallow baseline, the central claim of the experiment is not testable from the proposed design. The conclusion 'the transformer outperforms engineered features' cannot be interpreted without knowing how much of the gap is explained by text access alone.
